I won the treble in 2010.
footballers.story: Brazil's no-nonsense centre-back from the 2002 World Cup-winning team. Lúcio formed a defensive partnership with Roque Júnior that stood firm in Japan/Korea under Big Phil Scolari. Career: Internacional, Bayer Leverkusen (famously losing the Bundesliga, Champions League and DFB-Pokal finals in 2002, the 'Neverkusen' team), Bayern Munich (multiple Bundesliga titles), Inter Milan under Mourinho (treble 2010), Juventus, São Paulo, Palmeiras, FC Goa, Brasiliense. 105 Brazil caps, two Copa Americas, three World Cups. Underrated outside Germany and Italy.
didYouKnow: Lost three finals in 2002: Bundesliga, Champions League and DFB-Pokal, the 'Neverkusen' curse.
Eight years later, won the Champions League final for Inter against his old club Bayern Munich.
Scored a long-range free-kick in the 2010 Champions League quarter-final against CSKA Moscow.

footballers.name: Tim Krul
clues: I played at the 2014 World Cup, playing 1 game and conceding 0 goals.
I won the Championship with Norwich City in 2019, after a season-long loan that became permanent.
I joined Newcastle United at 17 from ADO Den Haag, and made over 150 Premier League appearances for them.
I was substituted on in the 120th minute of a World Cup quarter-final, and saved two penalties.
I'm a Dutch goalkeeper with 15 international caps, mostly playing back-up to Cillessen.
footballers.story: Football's most famous one-minute World Cup hero. Van Gaal substituted starting keeper Cillessen ON for Krul in the 120th minute of extra time of the 2014 quarter-final vs Costa Rica, purely for the penalty shootout. Krul saved two. Career: Newcastle United (10+ years, including their best Premier League season as 5th in 2012), Norwich (Premier League and Championship years), AZ Alkmaar, Luton Town. Quietly excellent shot-stopper, never a giant, but a top-half-of-the-Premier-League career.
didYouKnow: Van Gaal's WC 2014 sub-on for penalties was a masterstroke. Krul had been training for it.
Had loans at Carlisle United and Falkirk before he ever played for Newcastle.
Played for 10 different clubs across his career, including five loan spells before age 22.

footballers.name: Nicolas Anelka
clues: I played at the 2010 World Cup, playing 1 game and scoring 0 goals for my country.
I won a Champions League but also lost a Champions League final.
I was sent home from a World Cup after a row with my coach.
I have won the Premier League with two different clubs.
I took the deciding penalty in the 2008 Champions League final for Chelsea against Manchester United, and missed.
footballers.story: Football's 'Le Sulk', the gifted French striker whose career took him through every elite club without ever feeling at home. Arsenal (£500k from PSG, sold for £22m to Real Madrid 18 months later, funding the new training ground), Real Madrid (CL 2000), back to PSG, Liverpool, Manchester City, Fenerbahçe, Bolton (genuinely loved his Bolton spell), Chelsea (Premier League 2010), Shanghai Shenhua, Juventus, West Brom, Mumbai City. The famous 2010 World Cup row with Domenech, sent home, French team went on strike, Anelka banned 18 games. Fast, sullen, talented, never satisfied.
didYouKnow: The Arsenal sale to Real Madrid (£500k to £22m) bankrolled Arsenal's training ground at London Colney.
Sentenced to a 5-game ban for an antisemitic 'quenelle' celebration at West Brom in 2014.
Played for 13 clubs in 19 years, including four in his last three seasons.

footballers.name: Sofyan Amrabat
clues: I played in two World Cups (2018 and 2022), playing 9 games and scoring 0 goals. I am here again at the 2026 World Cup.
I was born in the Netherlands and represented Dutch youth teams before switching to my parents' country at senior level.
I was named Player of the Match against Spain in the 2022 World Cup Round of 16, when my country won on penalties.
I later moved on loan to Manchester United.
I was the heartbeat of the Moroccan side that became the first African team ever to reach a World Cup semi-final.
footballers.story: The Dutch-born Moroccan destroyer who emerged as one of the tournament's breakout stars at Qatar 2022. Career: Utrecht, Feyenoord, Club Brugge, Hellas Verona, Fiorentina (Conference League runners-up 2023), Manchester United (loan, didn't quite work), Fenerbahçe. The semi-final WC run with Morocco (beating Spain and Portugal en route) cemented his cult status, he ran 80km over the seven games. The reluctant heir to N'Golo Kanté's 'everywhere all at once' archetype.
didYouKnow: Born in Huizen, Netherlands; represented Dutch youth teams before switching to Morocco.
Older brother Nordin is also a footballer with Watford, Feyenoord and Anderlecht spells.
Was on Liverpool's transfer shortlist before the Manchester United loan deal.

footballers.name: Giorgio Chiellini
clues: I played in two World Cups (2010 and 2014), playing 6 games and scoring 0 goals.
I lost two Champions League finals with Juventus.
I was famously bitten in a World Cup.
I controversially fouled a player in a European Championship final.
I was awarded an Italian state honour, knighted by President Mattarella, for winning Euro 2020.
footballers.story: Italy's iron centre-back captain who won nine consecutive Serie A titles with Juventus (2012-2020) and lifted Euro 2020 in his late thirties. Career: Livorno (youth), Fiorentina (loan), Juventus (16 years), then LA Galaxy MLS retirement years. Famously the bite-victim of Luis Suárez at WC 2014, and the shirt-puller of Bukayo Saka at the Euro 2020 final. Italy: missed Euro 2016 through injury, won Euro 2020, missed WC 2018 and 2022. One of the best defenders of his generation, beloved at Juve. Now Juventus board member.
didYouKnow: Has a Masters degree in Business Administration which he completed while playing.
Lost two Champions League finals with Juventus (2015, 2017).
Pulled Bukayo Saka's shirt in the 65th minute of the Euro 2020 final, conceding a yellow card.

footballers.name: Pepe
clues: I played in four World Cups (2010, 2014, 2018 and 2022), playing 13 games and scoring 1 goal.
I scored in my last World Cup against Switzerland at age 39, becoming the oldest player ever to score in a World Cup knockout match.
I won three Champions Leagues.
I was banned for 10 games for an attack on a player in La Liga, including a stamp while he lay on the ground.
I was known as one of the most physical and dirty players in European football, and Mourinho loved me.
footballers.story: Brazil-born, Portugal-adopted, four-World-Cup centre-back. Marítimo, Porto (2 league titles), Real Madrid 2007-2017 (3 Champions Leagues), Beşiktaş, Porto again. Euro 2016 winner, 141 Portugal caps. Controversial in his Real Madrid years (red cards, on-pitch incidents), redeemed later as the experienced anchor of multiple Portugal generations. Played until age 41 at Porto. Often a Sergio Ramos counterpoint: the dirty cop.
didYouKnow: Held the record for oldest goalscorer in a WC knockout match at 39 years, 283 days.
Suspended for 10 games for stamping on Casquero in 2009.
Born in Maceió, Brazil; moved to Portugal at 18 to play for Marítimo.

footballers.name: Shkodran Mustafi
clues: I played at the 2014 World Cup, playing 1 game and scoring 0 goals.
I ended my career in Italy, where I'd also started it.
I was signed for £35 million by a Premier League club. It was not a success.
I won the 2014 World Cup, but hardly played.
I won the FA Cup twice, under both Wenger and Arteta.
footballers.story: The German centre-back who won the 2014 World Cup as a back-up, then earned the dubious honour of being the symbol of Arsenal's transfer-window decline. Career: Hamburg/Everton youth (released without a first-team game), Sampdoria, Valencia (Europa League final 2014), Arsenal (£35m, controversial buy, FA Cup wins, became a meme), Schalke, Levante, Como. Germany: WC 2014 winner (1 game), WC 2018 (group stage out). Always more competent than internet meme culture suggests, but never quite vindicated.
didYouKnow: Released by Everton at 19, rebuilt his career from Serie B with Sampdoria.
Was Wenger's last £30m+ signing and one of his most-criticised.
Has Albanian heritage; born in Bad Hersfeld, Germany.

footballers.name: Kalidou Koulibaly
clues: I played in two World Cups (2018 and 2022), playing 6 games and scoring 1 goal. I'm here again at the 2026 World Cup.
I spent eight years at Napoli before a £33 million move to a Premier League club in 2022.
I scored in the 2022 AFCON final penalty shootout against Egypt, which we went on to win for our first ever AFCON title.
I was born in France but represent the country my parents emigrated from at international level.
I scored the goal that put my country into the Round of 16 at the 2022 World Cup, against Ecuador.
footballers.story: Senegal's commanding centre-back captain. Born in Saint-Dié, France, raised in the Vosges region, chose to represent his parents' Senegal. Career: Metz youth, Genk (Belgian title), Napoli (8 years, two Serie A runners-up, near-misses against Juventus), Chelsea (one underwhelming season), Al-Hilal Saudi. Senegal: AFCON winner 2022 (captain), WC 2018 group stage exit, WC 2022 Round of 16 (scored vs Ecuador). One of the most respected defenders of the modern era.
didYouKnow: First Senegalese captain to lift the AFCON.
Has a degree in Sports Science from a French university.
The Genk team he won the Belgian title with also included Thibaut Courtois and Kevin De Bruyne.

footballers.name: Gennaro Gattuso
clues: I played in two World Cups (2002 and 2006), playing 11 games and scoring 0 goals.
I won the Champions League twice, but famously lost one in Istanbul.
I met my wife while playing football in Scotland.
I headbutted the Tottenham Assistant Manager in 2011, and was known for my physical style of play.
I won the 2006 World Cup, playing with Pirlo in the midfield.
footballers.story: Italy's snarling defensive midfielder. Gattuso was the destroyer in Cannavaro's WC 2006-winning team and Pirlo's protector at AC Milan for over a decade. Perugia, Rangers (Scottish football cult years), AC Milan (12 years, 2x Champions League 2003 + 2007, Serie A 2004 + 2011), Sion. 73 Italy caps. Later coached at Milan, Napoli, Valencia, Marseille (sacked for results), Hajduk Split. The original '100% commitment, 70% talent' archetype. Famously head-butted Joe Jordan on the touchline in 2011.
didYouKnow: Played for Rangers as a teenager and never lost his Glaswegian accent.
Head-butted Tottenham assistant Joe Jordan during a Champions League match in 2011.
His wife is Scottish; they met during his Rangers spell.

footballers.name: Daniele De Rossi
clues: I played in three World Cups (2006, 2010 and 2014), playing 9 games and scoring 2 goals.
I came on as a substitute in a World Cup final and converted one of the penalties in the shootout that won the trophy.
I have a World Cup winner's medal but I never won my own domestic league title.
I spent eighteen years at one Italian club in the capital, then flew to Argentina to live out a boyhood dream for a few months.
Wearing number 16, I captained the side from Rome for the best part of a decade.
footballers.story: Roma to the bone. De Rossi spent 18 years in the Giallorossi shirt, a snarling, cultured midfielder who could thread a pass and break up play with equal venom. He won the 2006 World Cup with Italy at 22, despite a four-match ban for elbowing McBride, and went on to captain his country. A Coppa Italia and two Italian Supercups were all Roma could give him, but his loyalty became legend. A late-career sabbatical at Boca Juniors fulfilled a boyhood dream, and a brief, doomed spell as Roma manager closed the loop on a one-man civic institution.
footballers.numbers: 2 goals in 9 World Cup games for Italy

footballers.name: Steven Nzonzi
clues: I played at the 2018 World Cup, playing 3 games and scoring 0 goals.
I have a World Cup winner's medal but I only started one World Cup match.
After I lifted the World Cup, my national team manager never picked me again.
I came on early in the second half of a World Cup final, and my captain later said I changed the game.
I was once Stoke City's player of the year, anchoring midfield at a famously cold ground.
footballers.story: The languid Frenchman with the Afro who walked into Sevilla and walked out a Europa League winner and a World Cup champion. Nzonzi started his English life at Blackburn and Stoke, where his composure on the ball stood out in a side built for chaos. Sevilla turned him into a genuinely elite holding midfielder, and Didier Deschamps rewarded him with a spot in France's 2018 squad, where he came on in the final against Croatia. Roma, Galatasaray and Rennes followed, but Russia 2018 is the line on his CV that does the talking.
footballers.numbers: 0 goals in 3 World Cup games for France

footballers.name: Sami Khedira
clues: I played in three World Cups (2010, 2014 and 2018), playing 15 games and scoring 2 goals.
I scored my country's fifth goal on the night they beat the hosts seven-one in a World Cup semi-final.
I was named in the starting eleven for a World Cup final, but pulled up injured in the warm-up minutes before kick-off.
I won the Champions League with my Spanish club just weeks before lifting the World Cup that same summer.
I won four straight Italian league titles after leaving Madrid for Turin.
footballers.story: The engine of two giants. Khedira broke through at Stuttgart, winning the Bundesliga in 2007, then spent five years at Real Madrid where he was a key part of La Decima in 2014. Weeks later he lifted the World Cup with Germany in the Maracana, the perfect summer. Juventus snapped him up on a free and he won four straight Scudetti in Turin, the metronome alongside Pirlo and later Pjanic. Injuries chipped away at the back end, but a brief Hertha Berlin send-off closed a career most midfielders would trade their boots for.
footballers.numbers: 2 goals in 15 World Cup games for Germany

footballers.name: Mats Hummels
clues: I played in three World Cups (2010, 2014 and 2018), playing 16 games and scoring 3 goals.
I headed in my country's second goal of the 2014 World Cup, against Portugal in the opening match.
I've played in two Champions League finals more than a decade apart, and lost both of them.
I moved between Germany's two giants in opposite directions, and won league titles at each of them.
I lifted the World Cup at the Maracanã wearing the number 5 shirt, and my best years were spent in yellow.
footballers.story: The thinking man's centre-back. Hummels came through Bayern's academy but made his name at Dortmund, where he was central to Klopp's back-to-back Bundesliga titles and the 2013 Champions League final run. His header won Germany the 2014 World Cup quarter-final against France, on the way to lifting the trophy in Brazil. A move home to Bayern brought three more league titles before he returned to Dortmund. Famously left out of Low's 2018 World Cup squad, he had the last laugh by clambering back into the side for Euro 2020.
footballers.numbers: 3 goals in 16 World Cup games for Germany

footballers.name: Luca Toni
clues: I played at the 2006 World Cup, playing 6 games and scoring 2 goals.
I had a header disallowed for offside in the World Cup final, and hit the crossbar with another effort.
I'm a World Cup winner, a European Golden Shoe winner, and a Bundesliga top scorer.
I scored 31 league goals in a single Serie A season and won the European Golden Shoe.
I'm 6 feet 4 inches tall, and I scored a hat-trick on my Bundesliga debut.
footballers.story: A late bloomer who never stopped scoring. Toni bounced around the Italian lower leagues into his late twenties before exploding at Palermo and then Fiorentina, where his 31 league goals in 2005-06 won him the European Golden Shoe. Months later he was a World Cup winner with Italy. Bayern Munich came calling and he delivered a Bundesliga and DFB-Pokal double in his first season. The fairy-tale ending arrived at Verona, where at 37 he won the Capocannoniere, the oldest top scorer in Serie A history, complete with his trademark ear-twist celebration.
footballers.numbers: 2 goals in 6 World Cup games for Italy

footballers.name: Robbie Keane
clues: I was born on the 8th of July 1980. I played 146 times for my country, scoring 68 goals.
I am the London club's record league scorer of the Premier League era for a foreign-born striker.
I had an unhappy spell in Serie A before bouncing back in Yorkshire, playing up front alongside players such as Mark Viduka and Alan Smith.
I captained my country at a World Cup where our talisman went home before a ball was kicked.
My celebration involved a forward roll and pretending to fire two pistols into the sky, and I did it on four continents.
footballers.story: Ireland's all-time top scorer with 68 goals and a career that read like a tour of European football. Keane was a teenage sensation at Wolves, then bounced through Coventry, an unhappy Inter spell and Leeds before finding his home at Tottenham, where he formed a brilliant partnership with Berbatov. A short, awkward Liverpool stint followed, then a loan back to Villa and the move that defined his late career: LA Galaxy, where he won two MLS Cups alongside Beckham and Donovan. The somersault celebration was the constant across all of it.
footballers.numbers: 68 goals in 146 caps for Republic of Ireland

footballers.name: Heurelho Gomes
clues: I was born on the 15th of February 1981. I played 11 times for my country, scoring 0 goals.
My first name is one of the strangest in football, a pure invention of my grandfather that nobody else on Earth shares.
My early years in the Premier League were so rough that pundits used my name as shorthand for goalkeeping panic.
At Tottenham I was eventually displaced by Brad Friedel and quietly slipped down the pecking order.
At Watford I became a folk hero by the end of my career, the most loved goalkeeper in the club's modern history.
footballers.story: Brazilian, eccentric, beloved. Heurelho Gomes won three Eredivisie titles in goal for PSV before a high-profile move to Tottenham where his shot-stopping was elite and his handling was, charitably, an adventure. Spurs fans never quite forgave him; Watford fans absolutely did. He became a Vicarage Road folk hero across nearly a decade, lifting the Hornets through promotion, an FA Cup final and relegation alike. He briefly retired in 2020, only to come back later the same week when Watford realised they still needed him. Few keepers have been more loved.

footballers.name: Damien Duff
clues: I was born on the 2nd of March 1979. I played 100 times for my country, scoring 8 goals.
I won two Premier Leagues in three seasons under José Mourinho, playing on the wing.
Although I won those titles at Chelsea, I played more games for Blackburn and Fulham.
I starred at the 2002 World Cup, dazzling defenders with my dribbling in the run to the last 16, before eventually going out on penalties to Spain.
My 100th cap for the Republic of Ireland made me one of the most decorated wingers in the country's history.
footballers.story: A scruffy, hard-running Irish winger with a low centre of gravity and a knack for the big moment. Damien Duff was the heartbeat of the Blackburn side that survived and then lifted the 2002 League Cup, before Chelsea paid £17m and he won back-to-back Premier Leagues under Jose Mourinho. Newcastle followed, then Fulham, where he scored in the 2010 Europa League final. He saw out his playing days in Melbourne and at Shamrock Rovers, before returning to manage the Hoops to back-to-back League of Ireland titles.

footballers.name: Matt Le Tissier
clues: I was born on the 14th of October 1968. I played 8 times for my country, scoring 0 goals.
When Xavi Hernández was asked to name the most technically gifted English footballer he had ever watched, he picked me, and the clip still resurfaces online every few years.
I scored a televised volley against Newcastle that the BBC named goal of the season, lobbing the keeper from outside the box after flicking the ball over a defender.
I am still the all-time leading scorer for the only senior club I ever represented, with two hundred and nine goals across sixteen years.
I scored the final ever competitive goal at The Dell, and my testimonial there packed the stand one last time.
footballers.story: Le God. The most gifted English footballer of his generation never played for a top-six club, refusing every approach to stay at Southampton, where he scored 209 goals from midfield in 16 years. He converted 47 of 48 penalties, a record that will never be broken, and produced a highlight reel of volleys, lobs and dribbles that still feels unreal. England capped him eight times, a national scandal. A divisive post-football turn into conspiracy theories has soured his public image, but on the pitch he was pure magic.

footballers.name: Clarence Seedorf
clues: I was born on the 1st of April 1976. I played 87 times for my country, scoring 11 goals.
I played for Ajax, Real Madrid, Inter Milan, and AC Milan, winning the league in Holland, Spain and Italy.
I am the only player ever to win the European Cup with three different clubs, in three different countries.
I missed a penalty for the Dutch team at Euro 2000, a moment I have never quite shaken off.
I moved across Milan in 2002 and stayed for a decade, lifting the Champions League twice more in red and black.
footballers.story: The only player to win the Champions League with three different clubs, and it might as well have been four. Seedorf lifted the trophy with Ajax in 1995, Real Madrid in 1998, and Milan in 2003 and 2007, a record that will probably never be broken. Add an Inter chapter and you have a man who played for four of European football's biggest names. A cultured, powerful midfielder with a piledriver of a long-range shot, he was a Dutch institution for nearly two decades. The trophies tell you everything.

footballers.name: Ruud van Nistelrooy
clues: I was born on the 1st of July 1976. I played 70 times for my country, scoring 35 goals.
I have served as a temporary manager at the Premier League club I famously played for.
I signed for Real Madrid in 2006 and won the Pichichi in my first season at the Bernabéu.
I had a bust-up with a young Portuguese winger over training-ground free kicks.
I was signed by Sir Alex Ferguson in 2001 and spent five years scoring 150 goals at Old Trafford for Manchester United.
footballers.story: A born finisher who started life as a defender, suffered a cruciate injury on the eve of his Manchester United move, came back and tore the Premier League apart. Ruud van Nistelrooy banged in 150 goals at Old Trafford, then won the Pichichi twice at Real Madrid before winding down at Málaga and Hamburg. PSV had launched him; Eindhoven welcomed him home. In management he has cut a steady figure with Burnley, a Manchester United caretaker stint and Leicester, the penalty-box predator turned thoughtful coach.

footballers.name: Walter Samuel
clues: I was born on the 23rd of March 1978. I played 56 times for my country, scoring 5 goals.
I won the Copa Libertadores and Intercontinental Cup with Boca Juniors as an imposing centre back before crossing the Atlantic.
I left Roma in 2004 for a 25 million euro deal to move to Real Madrid, but I returned to Italy to join Inter Milan after a failed season.
I won a treble under José Mourinho.
I formed a defensive partnership with the Brazilian known simply as Lúcio.
footballers.story: The Wall. Samuel was as hard as the nickname suggested, a no-frills Argentine centre-back who built his reputation at Boca Juniors and then Roma, where he won the 2001 Scudetto. Real Madrid bought him for big money but used him for just one season, and Inter swooped to take him to Milan, where he became the rock of Mourinho's 2010 treble-winning side. He played into his late thirties and won five straight Serie A titles with Inter. Argentina's defensive certainty across a decade, the kind of defender strikers genuinely did not enjoy facing.

footballers.name: Olof Mellberg
clues: I was born on the 3rd of September 1977. I played 117 times for my country, scoring 8 goals.
I was paired in central defence with Denmark's Martin Laursen for one of the most underrated partnerships of the Premier League era.
I grew up in a small Swedish town but made my name at Aston Villa.
I scored a brace against England at Euro 2012, in a 3-2 group-stage defeat that turned out to be my last act in a Sweden shirt.
I left for Italy on a Bosman in 2008, signing for Juventus at the age of thirty.
footballers.story: Seven years as Aston Villa's captain made him a Premier League fixture, a no-nonsense Swedish centre-back who threw himself at everything. Mellberg left Villa on a Bosman for Juventus in 2008, then won league titles at Olympiakos before a swansong at Villarreal and MLS side Chivas USA. For Sweden he was an immovable presence across four major tournaments, and at Euro 2012 he scored a brace against England in Kyiv at the age of 34, a reminder that some defenders only get better with age.

footballers.name: Yaya Touré
clues: I was born on the 13th of May 1983. I played 100 times for my country, scoring 19 goals.
My agent once said I was upset because nobody at my club had wished me a happy birthday, and the story spiralled into a global meme about cake.
I won a Champions League playing as an emergency centre-back in a final, filling in for a suspended teammate.
I won three Premier League titles, scoring 20 league goals in one season from midfield.
I was named African Footballer of the Year four years in a row, a record that still stands.
footballers.story: A force of nature. Yaya emerged at Barcelona, where Guardiola memorably played him at centre-back in the 2009 Champions League final, then moved to Manchester City and became one of the Premier League's most devastating midfielders. Three league titles, including the famous Aguero day in 2012 when he was central to the run-in, and 20 league goals in 2013-14. With Ivory Coast he finally lifted the Africa Cup of Nations in 2015 after years of agonising near-misses. African Footballer of the Year four times running. A true one-off.
footballers.numbers: 19 goals in 100 caps for Ivory Coast

footballers.name: Yossi Benayoun
clues: I was born on the 5th of May 1980. I played 102 times for my country, scoring 23 goals.
I played for West Ham, Liverpool, Chelsea, and Arsenal consecutively.
When Juan Mata signed for Chelsea in 2011, I gave him my number 10 shirt.
I was booed in 2011 during a friendly in Malaysia because of my nationality.
I am the most-capped player in my country's history and operated as an attacking midfielder.
footballers.story: Israel's most gifted modern footballer, a wandering playmaker who turned up everywhere. Yossi Benayoun left Hapoel Be'er Sheva for the Ajax youth academy before settling at Racing Santander, then dazzled in England with West Ham, Liverpool, Chelsea, an Arsenal loan and finally QPR. He won a Champions League winner's medal at Stamford Bridge and a Europa League at Chelsea, while captaining Israel to 102 caps. A footballer's footballer with the softest of touches and one of the kindest reputations in the dressing room.

footballers.name: Lucas Moura
clues: I was born on the 13th of August 1992. I played 38 times for my country, scoring 4 goals.
I was signed from São Paulo by PSG as their club-record signing, but never really made it there.
I lost a Champions League final for a club that has only made it there once.
My last-minute winner against Ajax for Spurs in the Champions League remains one of the most iconic moments in the club's history.
I joined PSG in 2013 for around forty-five million euros, then a Brazilian record fee.
footballers.story: A Brazilian winger who left a hat-trick in football's collective memory. Lucas Moura broke through at São Paulo, became Paris Saint-Germain's club-record signing in 2013, and then moved to Tottenham, where he spent five mostly tidy years. Then came Amsterdam, 2019, Champions League semi-final, three goals in the second half against Ajax to send Spurs to the final on away goals, including the last-second winner. He returned to São Paulo to finish at the club that built him, but those 45 minutes are immortal.

footballers.name: Memphis Depay
clues: I was born on the 13th of February 1994. I played 102 times for my country, scoring 51 goals.
I won the Eredivisie golden boot the year my club lifted the title, then left for England at twenty-one.
I have a rap career that the maker of this game has no idea is good or not.
I joined Manchester United from PSV in 2015 and lasted eighteen months at Old Trafford.
I am the all-time top scorer in my country's history.
footballers.story: A swaggering, tattooed Dutch forward who has had at least three careers. Memphis Depay won the Eredivisie Golden Boot at PSV in 2014-15, flopped at Manchester United at 21, and rebuilt himself into a complete attacker at Lyon. Barcelona snapped him up on a free, Atlético Madrid followed, and a move to Corinthians took him to Brazil. Along the way he became the Netherlands' all-time leading scorer in 2025, passing Robin van Persie, the swagger justified at last. Quiet on the pitch, very much not off it.

footballers.name: Andy Carroll
clues: I was born on the 6th of January 1989. I played 9 times for my country, scoring 2 goals.
I was signed to replace a Spanish striking legend who had been sold to Chelsea.
I came through at my hometown club on the Tyne and made my debut at seventeen.
My ponytail did more for tabloid photographers than any of my goals ever did.
I powered in a header for England against Sweden at a European Championship in Kiev to put us ahead.
footballers.story: A long-haired, headband-wearing throwback, the most expensive British footballer in history for about a fortnight. Andy Carroll came through Newcastle and forced a £35m move to Liverpool in January 2011, where the goals never quite flowed. West Ham, first on loan then permanently, became his home; he then drifted via Newcastle again, Reading and Bordeaux. He scored a brilliant header against Sweden at Euro 2012 and made the world remember exactly what English centre-forwards used to do. Injuries took the rest.

footballers.name: Nwankwo Kanu
clues: I was born on the 1st of August 1976. I played 87 times for my country, scoring 13 goals.
I won a Champions League in Italy with a club from the north before a move to London.
I scored the only goal of an FA Cup final at Wembley for a south-coast club under Harry Redknapp in 2008, my second winners' medal in the competition.
I was discovered at a junior tournament in Africa and signed by a Dutch giant before I had finished growing into my frame.
At six foot five I was once described as moving like a giraffe wearing roller skates, and I had a step-over that defied physics.
footballers.story: A 6'5" magician with feet of silk. Kanu won the Champions League with Ajax at 19, then heart surgery at Inter almost ended everything. Arsenal took the gamble, and he repaid them by scoring a 15-minute hat-trick at Chelsea and becoming a key squad member in the Invincibles season. Portsmouth was his late-career home, where he scored the winner in the 2008 FA Cup final. Add an Olympic gold with Nigeria in 1996 and two African Footballer of the Year awards and you have one of the most decorated Africans ever to play the game.

footballers.name: Aaron Ramsey
clues: I was born on the 26th of December 1990. I played 86 times for my country, scoring 22 goals.
I scored the winner in two FA Cup finals for a London club, both from close range, one of them in extra time.
A horror tackle at the Britannia almost ended me before I turned twenty.
I won Serie A with Juventus.
I captained Wales to a semi-final at our first major tournament in nearly sixty years.
footballers.story: The Cardiff academy product who became Arsenal's big-game man. Aaron Ramsey spent a decade in north London, winning two FA Cups and scoring the winning goal in both finals, the second one a sumptuous backheel. He survived a horror leg break at Stoke in 2010 to come back better. Juventus took him on a free, then Rangers loaned him in for a wild SPL season, Nice gave him a French stint, and a homecoming move to Cardiff and a spell at Pumas UNAM followed. He captained Wales to the 2016 Euros semi-finals, the country's best modern tournament.

footballers.name: Aaron Lennon
clues: I was born on the 16th of April 1987. I played 21 times for my country, scoring 0 goals.
My acceleration on the wing over the first ten yards was once measured as among the fastest in the league.
I played for England against Algeria in a game that the creator of this app has not really mentally recovered from.
I scored a late equaliser in a North London derby, a 4-4 draw that is still remembered.
My best years were as a flying right winger at Tottenham, supplying crosses for Berbatov, Defoe, and Bale.
footballers.story: A blur of pace on the right wing, picked up from Leeds' youth ranks. Lennon spent a decade at Tottenham, a key part of Harry Redknapp's Champions League side and the Bale-era teams, with 21 England caps along the way. Everton and Burnley provided steady late-career years, but the most important thing he did happened off the pitch. Detained under the Mental Health Act in 2017, he came back and became one of football's most articulate voices on mental health. Brave in a way the position never demanded.

footballers.name: Michael Ballack
clues: I was born on the 26th of September 1976. I played 98 times for my country, scoring 42 goals.
The year 2002 was the cruellest of my career: I finished runner-up in the league, the cup, the Champions League and the World Cup.
I won three doubles in four years at Bayern Munich before deciding I wanted to test myself in England.
I signed for Chelsea on a free in 2006 and won the Premier League, three FA Cups and reached a Champions League final in Moscow.
I grew up in Chemnitz when it was still called Karl-Marx-Stadt, and I started out at the local club before anyone in the west had heard of me.
footballers.story: The midfielder who collected runners-up medals like other players collected winners' ones. Michael Ballack came of age scoring the own goal that helped Kaiserslautern to a shock 1998 Bundesliga title, then became the heartbeat of Bayer Leverkusen's heartbreak 2002 side that lost the league, cup and Champions League final inside a fortnight. At Bayern he finally won three doubles, and a free transfer to Chelsea brought Premier League titles and FA Cups. Kevin-Prince Boateng's tackle in 2010 robbed him of a final World Cup, the cruel coda to a brilliant, near-miss career.

footballers.name: Ledley King
clues: I was born on the 12th of October 1980. I played 21 times for my country, scoring 2 goals.
Managers said that on one leg I was still better than most defenders on two.
I spent my entire career at a London club.
My most frequent centre-back partner was Michael Dawson.
I lifted the League Cup in 2008 with Tottenham, beating Chelsea in extra time, and it was the only major trophy of my career.
footballers.story: The defender who could barely train, and was still better than nearly everyone who could. Ledley King's knee cartilage was worn through by his mid-twenties, leaving him to play matches off little more than physio and adrenaline, often turning up only on Fridays. At Tottenham, where he spent his entire career, his reading of the game was so good that Thierry Henry called him the toughest defender he ever faced. He lifted the 2008 League Cup, his only major trophy, and remains the great Spurs what-if.

footballers.name: Wayne Bridge
clues: I was born on the 5th of August 1980. I played 36 times for my country, scoring 1 goal.
I was signed by both Chelsea and Manchester City at the beginning of their big spending eras.
I scored a famous winner at Highbury in a Champions League quarter-final.
I refused to shake hands with a former teammate in a pre-match line-up in February 2010, after a tabloid scandal involving my ex-partner.
I came through the Southampton academy as a classy attacking left back.
footballers.story: A classy, attacking left-back who came through the Southampton academy before Chelsea bought him in 2003. Bridge won two Premier League titles at Stamford Bridge in the Mourinho years, scoring a famous winner against Arsenal in the 2004 Champions League quarter-final. Manchester City signed him just as the oil money arrived, but he is sadly remembered most for refusing John Terry's handshake at Stamford Bridge in 2010, after the Terry-Vanessa Perroncel story broke. England capped him 36 times, and he loaned out his last seasons across the lower reaches of the league.

footballers.name: Bojan Krkić
clues: I was born on the 28th of August 1990. I played 1 time for my country, scoring 0 goals.
I was tipped to be the successor to the greatest footballer ever, becoming the youngest player to feature in a La Liga match for this club.
I played in the English Championship, having originally been signed by Mark Hughes.
I was in Pep Guardiola's Champions League final squad at Barcelona, although I did not get on the pitch.
Stoke City fans still sing about me, and I think about that little corner of England often.
footballers.story: Barcelona's youngest ever league scorer at 17, anointed as the next Messi before he had even started shaving. The pressure broke him. Bojan struggled with anxiety attacks and famously pulled himself out of Spain's Euro 2008 squad. He drifted on loan to Roma and Milan, then found something like peace at Stoke City, where he became a beloved cult hero in the Mark Hughes era before a serious knee injury derailed things. Mainz, Montreal and a coaching return to Barca rounded out a career that quietly carried more weight than most.

footballers.name: Andros Townsend
clues: I was born on the 16th of July 1991. I played 13 times for my country, scoring 3 goals.
I came through the Tottenham Hotspur academy and played for the first team for five seasons, but you will probably know me for my five years at Crystal Palace.
My long-range volley for a London club against Manchester City was the Premier League goal of the season in 2018-19.
I most recently played in the Premier League with Luton in a renaissance season.
I scored on my England debut against Montenegro at the old stadium and thought I'd cracked it.
footballers.story: A flying right winger and one of the great loan stories of his generation. Townsend went out nine times from Tottenham before he started a Premier League game, learning the trade at Yeovil, Millwall, Watford and Birmingham. He scored a thunderbolt on his England debut against Montenegro in 2013 at Wembley, then drifted from Spurs to Newcastle, Palace and Everton, where his Premier League goal-of-the-season volley against Manchester City in 2018-19 was the highlight. A serious knee injury at Goodison effectively closed the door before a Luton swansong.

footballers.name: Dimitar Berbatov
clues: I was born on the 30th of January 1981. I played 78 times for my country, scoring 48 goals.
I played in the 2002 Champions League final for a Bundesliga club, although I am not German.
I won two Premier League titles and a Golden Boot, sharing the latter with another striker on 20 goals.
I scored five goals in a single Premier League match against Blackburn, including a hat-trick in the second half.
My first touch was once described as "like a cat in slippers", and I rarely broke into a jog let alone a sprint.
footballers.story: Languid, brilliant, and seemingly above it all. Berbatov made his name at Bayer Leverkusen, including a run to the 2002 Champions League final, before Tottenham brought him to the Premier League. Two ludicrously good seasons in north London earned a club-record move to Manchester United, where he won two titles and shared the Golden Boot in 2010-11 with a five-goal haul against Blackburn. Fulham, Monaco, Greek football and even a stop in India followed. Bulgaria's all-time top scorer, with the touch of a man who had nothing to prove.

footballers.name: Wilfried Bony
clues: I was born on the 10th of December 1988. I played 59 times for my country, scoring 18 goals.
My January move to Manchester City made me one of the most expensive African footballers ever at the time.
I won a League Cup at one of the big six, but the transfer was undoubtedly a failure and I ended up back at the club I was sold from.
I was the top scorer in the calendar year of the Premier League at a club in Wales.
I joined the AFCON squad mid-tournament in 2015 after a transfer saga, and we won it.
footballers.story: A short, blunt-force striker who at his peak was unplayable. Bony scored 31 goals in his second season at Vitesse, then arrived at Swansea and proved it was no fluke, finishing as the Premier League's top scorer across the 2014 calendar year. Manchester City bought him in January 2015 after he had won the Africa Cup of Nations with Ivory Coast, and he came on to lift the League Cup. The City move never really worked, and loans and a return to Swansea followed, but the Premier League at its peak saw the real Bony.
footballers.numbers: 18 goals in 59 caps for Ivory Coast

footballers.name: Park Ji-sung
clues: I was born on the 25th of February 1981. I played 100 times for my country, scoring 13 goals.
My nickname translated as "Three Lung", because teammates swore I ran twice as far as anyone else on the pitch.
I scored in three consecutive World Cups, and captained my country at the third of them in South Africa.
I was deployed as a man-marker on AC Milan's playmaker in a Champions League knockout tie, and the Italian press dubbed me a destroyer.
I won three Premier League titles and was the first Asian player to lift the European Cup.
footballers.story: The lung-busting heartbeat of a great Manchester United side. Park caught the world's eye at the 2002 World Cup under Guus Hiddink, followed him to PSV and then to Old Trafford in 2005, where Sir Alex used him in the biggest games against the best opposition. He won three Premier League titles and started the 2008 Champions League final on the bench, but he made history by lifting the trophy the next year. South Korea's captain at the 2010 World Cup, and the first Asian player to win the Champions League. Loved everywhere he played.
footballers.numbers: 13 goals in 100 caps for South Korea

footballers.name: Mark Viduka
clues: I was born on the 9th of October 1975. I played 43 times for my country, scoring 11 goals.
I played in the 2001 Champions League semi-final loss to Valencia for Leeds, and the club's financial collapse later forced them to sell me.
I captained Australia in the 2005 shootout against Uruguay in Sydney that sent us to our first World Cup since 1974.
I scored four goals in a single Premier League match against Liverpool at Elland Road in 2000.
I am remembered as the most prolific Australian striker of my generation, a powerhouse number nine with surprising touch.
footballers.story: Big, brilliant and beloved. Mark Viduka left Dinamo Zagreb for Celtic, where he walked out on the club one game in citing depression, returned and started scoring for fun, then signed for Leeds in their Champions League pomp. He scored all four in a 4-3 win over Liverpool that fans still bore each other with. Middlesbrough got the best of him next, helped him to a UEFA Cup final, and Newcastle had the last chapter. He captained Australia at the 2006 World Cup, the most technically gifted Socceroos striker of his generation.

footballers.name: Owen Hargreaves
clues: I was born on the 20th of January 1981. I played 42 times for my country, scoring 0 goals.
I was born in Calgary to a Welsh father and an English mother, and I could have played for three countries.
I won the Champions League twice, the first time with Bayern Munich and the second time with a Premier League club.
I played for two Premier League clubs, Manchester United and Manchester City.
I came on as a substitute in the Moscow rain and helped win another Champions League trophy.
footballers.story: Calgary-born, Bayern-raised, England's most unusual midfielder of the 2000s. Owen Hargreaves came through at Bayern Munich, winning a Champions League at 19, and was arguably England's best player at the 2006 World Cup playing out of position at right-back. A blockbuster move to Manchester United brought a free-kick goal and a winners' medal in Moscow as a substitute in the 2008 final, before chronic tendinitis robbed him of his late twenties. A Manchester City free transfer offered one last cameo. A career of brilliance bookended by what might have been.

footballers.name: Yohan Cabaye
clues: I was born on the 14th of January 1986. I played 48 times for my country, scoring 4 goals.
The first four clubs of my career were Lille, Newcastle, PSG and Crystal Palace.
France called me up for Euro 2012 and the 2014 World Cup, where I helped my country reach the quarter-finals.
At Newcastle I was once voted the club's player of the season after a dazzling debut campaign in the Premier League.
At Newcastle United I formed a famously fluent partnership with Cheick Tioté in midfield.
footballers.story: A snappy, neat French midfielder who always seemed to play for a club punching above its weight. Yohan Cabaye won Ligue 1 and the Coupe de France in Lille's brilliant 2011 double, then moved to Newcastle, where his goals and passing carried a team that finished fifth. PSG bought him back to France, Crystal Palace took him to the FA Cup final, and Saint-Étienne gave him a final ride. He earned 48 France caps across Euro 2012 and the 2014 World Cup, the dependable engine no Didier Deschamps midfield was without.

footballers.name: Thomas Vermaelen
clues: I was born on the 14th of November 1985. I played 85 times for my country, scoring 2 goals.
I was part of the Arsenal squad that won the FA Cup at Wembley in 2014, ending a near nine-year trophy drought for the club.
I was part of Belgium's golden generation, lining up at three major tournaments through the 2010s.
I came up at Ajax in a defensive unit alongside Toby Alderweireld and Jan Vertonghen, all three of us future Premier League fixtures.
I captained Arsenal during the mid-2010s, with Mikel Arteta succeeding me when I left for Barcelona.
footballers.story: Captain of Arsenal, unused Champions League winner with Barcelona, and a man whose career was haunted by injuries. Vermaelen was outstanding at Ajax before Wenger brought him to north London, where he wore the armband and chipped in with crucial goals from the back. Barcelona was meant to be his crowning move, but he played barely a minute in his first season as they won the treble in 2015. Loans at Roma, a stint at Vissel Kobe alongside Iniesta, and a Belgium career that included third place at the 2018 World Cup added up nicely in the end.

footballers.name: Michael Carrick
clues: I was born on the 28th of July 1981. I played 34 times for my country, scoring 0 goals.
I won five Premier Leagues, one Champions League and a Europa League.
I won my first Premier League title in my debut season at my biggest club, having moved from Tottenham in 2006.
Sir Alex Ferguson once said signing me was one of the best decisions of his late career.
I am currently managing a Premier League club that I famously played for.
footballers.story: The metronome. Michael Carrick was one of West Ham's four amigos, did a quiet, classy season at Tottenham, and then spent 12 years at Manchester United changing tempo with a single pass. Five Premier League titles, a Champions League in Moscow 2008, a Europa League and just about everything else followed, yet he won only 34 England caps, a national oversight. Sir Alex Ferguson once said he was as good a passer as Paul Scholes. He returned to Old Trafford as a coach, briefly stepped up as head coach, and is now managing in his own right.

footballers.name: Marco Reus
clues: I was born on the 31st of May 1989. I played 48 times for my country, scoring 15 goals.
I never won a Bundesliga, and I have been a Champions League losing finalist twice with the same club, more than a decade apart.
I provided assists for both Lewandowski and Haaland at the same club.
I was voted German Footballer of the Year twice and Bundesliga Player of the Season on three occasions, all as an attacking midfielder.
I am still playing football, in LA.
footballers.story: The Borussia Dortmund icon who suffered for his loyalty. Marco Reus came home from Mönchengladbach in 2012 and stayed for 12 years, scoring relentlessly when fit, never quite winning the Bundesliga, watching teammate after teammate leave for Bavaria. A pre-tournament injury ruled him out of Germany's 2014 World Cup-winning squad in the cruellest possible timing. Two German Footballer of the Year awards say what trophies could not. He left Dortmund in 2024 for LA Galaxy, the cult hero finally allowed a curtain call abroad.

footballers.name: Esteban Cambiasso
clues: I was born on the 18th of April 1980. I played 52 times for my country, scoring 5 goals.
I won the 2010 Champions League in the centre of midfield.
I had a glorious one-season cameo at Leicester in England, helping a small club avoid relegation against the odds.
I won Serie A five times in a row for one of the Milan clubs.
I scored the famous 24-pass goal for Argentina at the 2006 World Cup, finishing off one of the greatest team moves ever seen.
footballers.story: A bald, anticipatory midfielder who made winning look like an administrative task. Esteban Cambiasso came through River Plate, won La Liga at Real Madrid while spending much of it on the bench, then moved to Inter and won five straight Scudetti, ending with Jose Mourinho's 2010 treble. Released at 34, he turned up at Leicester and was voted their player of the season in the 2014-15 great escape, the campaign before the title. He finished at Olympiakos, then went into coaching, the kind of player every dressing room wishes it had.

footballers.name: Adriano
clues: I was born on the 17th of February 1982. I played 48 times for my country, scoring 27 goals.
I finished in the top 10 of the Ballon d'Or in both 2004 and 2005, before winning Serie A in 2006.
I was on the cover of Pro Evolution Soccer 6.
I grew up in a Rio favela where playing barefoot on concrete left me with a left foot like a sledgehammer.
I returned to my boyhood club Flamengo and inspired them to a Brazilian title, before the off-field troubles caught up with me again.
footballers.story: L'Imperatore. For a few years in the mid-2000s Adriano was the most physically terrifying striker on the planet, a Brazilian battering ram with a left foot like a cannon who fired Inter to back-to-back Serie A titles. His Copa America in 2004 was one of the great individual tournaments. Then his father died, and the joy drained out of his football. He drifted home to Brazil, where a brilliant spell at Flamengo won him the 2009 Brazilian title and Player of the Year award, before the lower leagues and retirement. One of football's saddest what-ifs.

footballers.name: Jonathan Woodgate
clues: I was born on the 22nd of January 1980. I played 8 times for my country, scoring 0 goals.
I scored a cup final winner for Tottenham against Chelsea in the 2008 League Cup.
I came through the Leeds academy in the late 1990s and was a senior pro by the time they reached the Champions League semi-final.
My debut for Real Madrid is probably the most disastrous in history, an own goal and a red card on the same afternoon.
I had three separate spells at Middlesbrough and even managed the club for a season in the Championship.
footballers.story: An England centre-back of genuine class whose body would not cooperate. Jonathan Woodgate came through the Leeds academy in their Champions League years, moved to Newcastle and earned a glamour transfer to Real Madrid, where he marked his debut by scoring an own goal and being sent off. Injuries dogged him at Middlesbrough and then Tottenham, where he did head home the winning goal in the 2008 League Cup final. He later managed Boro and coached at Bournemouth, calmer in the dugout than his career ever allowed him to be on the pitch.

footballers.name: Florent Malouda
clues: I was born on the 13th of June 1980. I played 80 times for my country, scoring 9 goals.
I came on in a World Cup final and was involved in the move that led to a famous headbutt by my captain.
A move to west London cost 13 million pounds, and it took me a full season to silence the doubters.
I once finished a Premier League season with 12 goals from the left wing, in a title-winning team under Carlo Ancelotti.
I was born in French Guiana, a long way from any European football pyramid, and I still represented the territory in non-FIFA tournaments late in my career.
footballers.story: Born in French Guiana, raised on flair. Malouda became the heart of an outstanding Lyon side that won four straight Ligue 1 titles in the mid-2000s, then moved to Chelsea in 2007 and won the Premier League, the FA Cup and, in 2012, the Champions League in Munich. He scored in the final against Bayern. Spells in Turkey, Egypt, Belgium and India saw him chase the football around the world deep into his thirties, and France capped him 80 times, with a runners-up medal from the 2006 World Cup as a teammate of Zidane and co.

footballers.name: Dimitri Payet
clues: I was born on the 29th of March 1987. I played 38 times for my country, scoring 8 goals.
I was in the team of the tournament at UEFA Euro 2016, reaching the final.
I was in the PFA Team of the Year in 2016, despite not playing for one of the biggest clubs.
I had two spells at Marseille with a Premier League stint in between.
I scored a free kick for West Ham so good that the opposing manager applauded me on the touchline.
footballers.story: A late bloomer who became, for one extraordinary season, the most exciting player in the Premier League. Payet's 2015-16 at West Ham was full of free-kick wonder goals and ridiculous assists, earning him a place in the PFA Team of the Year and a starring role at Euro 2016 on home soil, where he scored against Romania in the opener. Twice a Marseille player, he had two great spells at the Velodrome either side of the West Ham fairy-tale. His exit from London, where he downed tools to force a return to France, soured the legacy but cannot dim the football.

footballers.name: Daley Blind
clues: I was born on the 9th of March 1990. I played 99 times for my country, scoring 2 goals.
I got to the semi-final of the 2014 World Cup, before being signed by the same manager for a Premier League club.
I could play left-back, centre-back, or sit in front of the back four, and managers loved me for it.
I won three Eredivisie titles before I left, and another one when I came home a decade later.
I spent four seasons at Old Trafford before going home to Ajax in 2018.
footballers.story: Footballing brain, footballing surname. Daley Blind played anywhere Ajax or Manchester United needed him: left-back, centre-back, defensive midfield, all with the same calm distribution learned from his father Danny. He won the Eredivisie multiple times at Ajax, played a key role in the 2014 World Cup semi-final run, and lifted the 2017 Europa League at Old Trafford. A heart condition diagnosed in 2019 ended for many but not him; he carried on, returning to Ajax to win another title, then taking a Bayern Munich loan and a stint at Girona.

footballers.name: Sami Hyypiä
clues: I was born on the 7th of October 1973. I played 105 times for my country, scoring 5 goals.
I won a cup treble in my second season in England, lifting the FA Cup, League Cup and UEFA Cup in one summer.
My country never qualified for a major tournament throughout my long international career as captain.
I formed a formidable centre-back partnership with a future Sky Sports pundit.
I won the Champions League final in Istanbul.
footballers.story: Liverpool's £2.6m bargain of the century. Houllier signed Hyypiä from Willem II in 1999 and got a decade of commanding, almost serene centre-back play in return. He was central to the 2001 cup treble and lifted the Champions League in Istanbul in 2005, one of the great captain's nights even if Gerrard wore the armband. Bayer Leverkusen gave him a Bundesliga sign-off, and he later managed them. Finland never reached a major tournament with him, which remains a small injustice given how much he carried that defence.

footballers.name: Stewart Downing
clues: I was born on the 22nd of July 1984. I played 35 times for my country, scoring 0 goals.
I played in a UEFA Cup final for my hometown club, losing to Sevilla in Eindhoven in 2006.
I joined Liverpool in 2011 for around 20 million pounds and endured a famously rough first season under Kenny Dalglish.
I returned to Middlesbrough for a third spell, helping the club back into the Premier League.
England fans will remember me from Euro 2012, where I played every minute of the group stage on the right wing.
footballers.story: A proper old-school English left winger whose career felt like a barometer of the early Premier League boom. Stewart Downing came through at Middlesbrough, helped them to a UEFA Cup final in 2006, and earned a big move to Aston Villa before Liverpool spent £20m on him in 2011, a deal that defined the post-Torres anxiety on Merseyside. He rebuilt at West Ham, looked like a genuinely top-class creator again, and finished by helping Boro back to the Premier League. Quiet, technically sound, and frequently underrated.

footballers.name: Fabien Barthez
clues: I was born on the 28th of June 1971. I played 87 times for my country, scoring 0 goals.
At one point I was the most expensive goalkeeping transfer in British football history.
I won the European Cup with Marseille.
I won both the World Cup and the European Championship with my country.
I won the Premier League in my first season at Manchester United.
footballers.story: The bald, charismatic Frenchman who won a Champions League at 21 with Marseille and never quite stopped being unpredictable. Fabien Barthez moved to Monaco, then Manchester United for a world-record fee for a goalkeeper, where his sweeping and ball-playing dazzled before the howlers crept in. He returned to France with Nantes, lifted the 1998 World Cup with Zinedine Zidane kissing his head for luck, and added Euro 2000 for good measure. Retirement brought a second career in motor racing, because of course it did.

footballers.name: Jermain Defoe
clues: I was born on the 7th of October 1982. I played 57 times for my country, scoring 20 goals.
I'm one of only a handful of players to score 150 plus goals in the English top flight.
I was one of those players that Harry Redknapp kept signing.
I formed one of the classic big-man-little-man partnerships with Peter Crouch.
I left Tottenham twice and came back twice, which says something about my feelings for the place.
footballers.story: A poacher's poacher who refused to fade away. Jermain Defoe burst out of West Ham, infamously biting Javier Mascherano along the way, then scored goals everywhere he went: two spells at Tottenham, Portsmouth, a Sunderland relegation fight that he very nearly won single-handed, Toronto, Bournemouth, and finally Rangers in the SPL. He finished with 162 Premier League goals. Above all that statistical noise sits his friendship with young Sunderland fan Bradley Lowery, an act of footballing love that endeared him to the country far beyond his goals.

footballers.name: David Villa
clues: I was born on the 3rd of December 1981. I played 98 times for my country, scoring 59 goals.
I top-scored at Euro 2008 with four goals, then had to watch the final from the bench after picking up an injury in the semi.
I won silverware in Spain with Valencia, Barcelona and Atlético Madrid.
I won the Champions League in my first season at the Camp Nou.
I am still my country's all-time top scorer, and I lifted the World Cup wearing the number 7 shirt.
footballers.story: Spain's all-time top scorer. El Guaje rose at Sporting Gijon, exploded at Valencia, then moved to Barcelona where he won La Liga, the Champions League and just about everything else under Guardiola. He scored five goals at the 2010 World Cup as Spain were crowned champions, and he had already won Euro 2008. Atletico Madrid gave him a famous final La Liga title in 2014 as Simeone's outsiders broke the duopoly. He finished up in MLS with NYCFC and at Vissel Kobe in Japan, scoring everywhere he went. A pure, ruthless centre-forward.

footballers.name: Henrik Larsson
clues: I was born on the 20th of September 1971. I played 106 times for my country, scoring 37 goals.
I won the Champions League, setting up both goals against Arsenal.
I played for Manchester United in a brief loan spell in 2007.
I was a prolific scorer in the United Kingdom, but not in the Premier League.
I scored for Sweden in the 1994 World Cup third-place match.
footballers.story: From stacking fruit in a Helsingborg warehouse to seven seasons of unrelenting goals at Celtic, 242 of them, Henrik Larsson built one of the great underrated careers. Feyenoord launched him; the Hoops adored him. A move to Barcelona at 32 brought a Champions League winner's medal in 2006, and the famous Thierry Henry line that "people speak about Ronaldinho and Eto'o but I think Larsson won the final". A loan stint at Manchester United enchanted Old Trafford, and he ended back at Helsingborg, eventually playing alongside his son Jordan.

footballers.name: Darren Bent
clues: I was born on the 6th of February 1984. I played 13 times for my country, scoring 4 goals.
I scored a goal that deflected in off an inflatable beach ball and the referee gave it.
In the 2009-10 season I scored 24 league goals, roughly half of Sunderland's total for the entire campaign.
I was a striker who played for Charlton, Tottenham, Sunderland and Aston Villa, scoring effectively at every one of them.
I was frequently close to making England squads for major tournaments but never made the final cut.
footballers.story: An English centre-forward who was steadily, unfussily excellent for the best part of a decade. Darren Bent came through Ipswich, banged them in for Charlton, then traded up to Tottenham, Sunderland (for a then-British record from Wearside), Aston Villa, Fulham, Brighton and Derby. He scored 106 Premier League goals despite playing for almost every mid-table side. He will, inevitably, always be the man whose deflected shot off a beach ball beat Pepe Reina at the Stadium of Light in 2009, the most ridiculous goal of the Premier League era.

footballers.name: Andrey Arshavin
clues: I was born on the 29th of May 1981. I played 75 times for my country, scoring 17 goals.
I became Arsenal's most expensive signing in history at the time of my arrival.
I scored four goals in a game.
I had a feud with a manager over fitness and weight which the tabloids loved to cover.
My number-23 shirt at Arsenal sold in huge numbers in Moscow the summer I signed.
footballers.story: The cheeky, broad-shouldered Russian who briefly lit up the world. Andrey Arshavin won the UEFA Cup with Zenit Saint Petersburg in 2008, then danced through Euro 2008 as Russia reached the semi-finals, drawing every big club in Europe. Arsenal got him for a club-record fee and, on a wild April night at Anfield, he scored four against Liverpool in a 4-4 draw. The brilliance was sporadic after that. He drifted back to Zenit, then Kuban, and finally Kairat in Kazakhstan, the smile never quite leaving him.

footballers.name: Joleon Lescott
clues: I was born on the 16th of August 1982. I played 26 times for my country, scoring 1 goal.
I lifted the Premier League trophy on the last day of the 2012 season.
My move to Merseyside in 2006 took a few seasons to convince the locals, but I left as a fan favourite.
I was hit by a car as a five-year-old and left with a fractured skull, but recovered to become a professional footballer.
I was bought by a Manchester club in a late-summer 2009 saga that involved my old club lodging a tapping-up complaint.
footballers.story: The defender who, as a child, was hit by a car and was told he might never play again. Lescott came through at Wolves, then established himself in the Premier League at Everton under David Moyes, where he was one of the best ball-playing centre-backs in the country. Manchester City paid big money in 2009 and he won two Premier League titles in the early Sheikh Mansour years, including the famous Aguero campaign of 2011-12. West Brom and Aston Villa rounded things off, and 26 England caps tell their own story.

footballers.name: Dwight Yorke
clues: I was born on the 3rd of November 1971. I played 74 times for my country, scoring 19 goals.
I formed one of the most famous striking partnerships in Premier League history.
I scored 17 league goals in a single season at a Midlands club, then doubled my haul in red after a club-record move.
I scored in both legs of a Champions League semi-final against Juventus, including a header in Turin that helped turn the tie.
I won the treble in 1999 at Old Trafford, and my goal-of-the-month against Arsenal in the FA Cup semi-final still gets replayed.
footballers.story: That smile. Yorke was the laid-back genius of Manchester United's 1999 Treble, forming a telepathic partnership with Andy Cole that fired them to the Premier League, FA Cup and Champions League in one of football's great seasons. He had emerged at Aston Villa under Graham Taylor and Brian Little, where his pace and finishing made him a Villa Park favourite. Blackburn, Birmingham, Sunderland and a fondly remembered spell at Sydney FC followed, where he was the marquee signing of the inaugural A-League. Forever Trinidad and Tobago's most famous footballer.
footballers.numbers: 19 goals in 74 caps for Trinidad and Tobago

footballers.name: Jamie Carragher
clues: I was born on the 28th of January 1978. I played 38 times for my country, scoring 0 goals.
As a child I supported the biggest local rival of the team I went on to play for my entire career.
I have scored seven own goals in my Premier League career, the second-most in the competition's history.
I retired from international football, then called into talkSPORT to push back at a pundit's criticism of me.
I played in two Champions League finals in three years, winning one and losing the other to a Milan side I'd already beaten.
footballers.story: A Bootle-born Evertonian who became one of Anfield's most decorated lifers. Jamie Carragher made over 700 appearances for Liverpool, all heart and positional intelligence, never quite quick but always in the right place. He was the cramping, defiant figure who refused to lose in Istanbul 2005, dragging Liverpool through a Champions League final that should not have been possible. FA Cups, League Cups and a UEFA Cup followed. Since hanging up the boots he has become Sky's sharpest analyst, the scouse half of the country's most quoted punditry double act.

footballers.name: Tomáš Rosický
clues: I was born on the 4th of October 1980. I played 105 times for my country, scoring 23 goals.
I won two FA Cups at Arsenal, although injuries restricted my appearances over a decade in north London.
I won the German League title in 2002.
I captained the Czech Republic at Euro 2012.
I scored a memorable volley against Sunderland in 2013 for Arsenal that won the Premier League goal of the season.
footballers.story: Little Mozart. Tomáš Rosický conducted Sparta Prague as a teenager, won the 2002 Bundesliga with Dortmund, and then spent a decade at Arsenal where his vision, scampering runs and Theatre of Dreams strike against Spurs lit up the rare seasons his hamstrings allowed. Injuries cost him whole years of his prime, but he never lost the elegance or the smile. He returned to Sparta to finish where he began, and remains the most-capped Czech outfield player of the modern era.
footballers.numbers: 23 goals in 105 caps for Czech Republic

footballers.name: Ricardo Carvalho
clues: I was born on the 18th of May 1978. I played 89 times for my country, scoring 5 goals.
Jose Mourinho once suggested I should take an IQ test, after I publicly questioned why he hadn't started me in a season opener.
I was named UEFA Club Defender of the Year in 2003–04.
I've been managed by Jose Mourinho at three different clubs.
I was stamped on by Wayne Rooney at the 2006 World Cup — he was sent off for it.
footballers.story: The most underrated centre-back of his generation. Won the Champions League with Porto, the Premier League three times with Chelsea, La Liga with Real Madrid — and Euro 2016 with Portugal at 38. Wherever Mourinho went, Carvalho followed.
footballers.numbers: 19 club goals in 433 appearances · 89 caps for Portugal
didYouKnow: Followed José Mourinho from Porto to Chelsea in 2004, then again from Inter to Real Madrid in 2010 — one of the great manager-player partnerships of the era.
Retired from international football in 2011 saying he'd been "disrespected", then came back nearly three years later and won Euro 2016 with Portugal at 38.
Was given a seven-month suspended prison sentence in Spain in 2017 for tax fraud during his Real Madrid years — a fate that befell several Spain-based stars of his era.

footballers.name: Cheick Tioté
clues: I was born on the 21st of June 1986. I played 52 times for my country, scoring 1 goal.
I represented my country at the 2010 and 2014 World Cups, and the 2012 and 2013 Africa Cup of Nations.
I scored arguably the most famous goal in my club’s modern history — a stunning volley to make it 4–4 against Arsenal, completing a comeback from 4–0 down.
I played alongside Yohan Cabaye and Hatem Ben Arfa in a midfield that gave Newcastle their best Premier League finish in years.
I tragically collapsed and died during a training session in June 2017 at the age of 30.
footballers.story: A warrior in the truest sense — Cheick Tioté played football like every tackle was a matter of honour. The Ivorian arrived at Newcastle from FC Twente in 2010 and quickly became the heartbeat of Alan Pardew's midfield, a ferocious ball-winner whose energy and aggression set the tone for everything the team did. His volley against Arsenal in February 2011 — completing a comeback from 4–0 down to draw 4–4 — remains arguably the greatest goal in Newcastle's modern history. He was part of the Ivory Coast squad at two World Cups and was capped 52 times. His death during a training session in China in June 2017, aged just 30, shocked the football world. Tyneside has never forgotten him.
honours: Africa Cup of Nations runner-up (Ivory Coast, 2012)
footballers.numbers: 1 goal in 52 caps for Ivory Coast
didYouKnow: His volley to make it 4–4 against Arsenal in February 2011 — completing a comeback from four goals down — is widely regarded as the greatest goal in Newcastle's Premier League history
He formed a formidable midfield partnership with Yohan Cabaye that powered Newcastle to fifth place in the 2011–12 season
He tragically collapsed and died during a training session with Beijing Enterprises in China in June 2017, aged just 30

footballers.name: Mauro Icardi
clues: I was born on the 19th of February 1993. I played 8 times for my country, scoring 1 goal.
I was once the top scorer in Serie A, finishing ahead of Gonzalo Higuaín and Edin Džeko.
Despite being one of the most prolific strikers in Serie A, I was barely capped by my country — largely due to off-pitch controversies.
I moved to Paris for around €50 million but was never the first-choice striker, spending much of my time on the bench.
I married the ex-wife of a former teammate — a scandal that dominated Italian football gossip for years.
footballers.story: One of the most prolific — and most controversial — strikers of his generation. Icardi was a clinical finisher who scored over 120 goals for Inter Milan, captaining the club and finishing as Serie A's top scorer in 2014–15. But the drama was never far away. His relationship with Wanda Nara — the ex-wife of former teammate Maxi Lopez — became one of Italian football's biggest soap operas and alienated him from dressing rooms and national team selectors alike. Despite his goalscoring record, Argentina capped him just eight times. A €50 million move to PSG in 2020 promised a fresh start, but he spent most of his time behind Mbappé and Neymar. A career defined equally by goals and gossip — few players have divided opinion so sharply.
didYouKnow: He was stripped of the Inter Milan captaincy in 2019 after a bitter contractual dispute that saw him frozen out of the squad for months
His marriage to Wanda Nara — the ex-wife of his former Sampdoria teammate Maxi Lopez — created one of Italian football's most infamous off-pitch scandals
Despite scoring over 120 goals for Inter, he won just 8 caps for Argentina — one of the most prolific strikers ever to be virtually ignored by his national team

footballers.name: Michael Essien
clues: I was born on the 3rd of December 1982. I played 59 times for my country, scoring 9 goals.
My teammates nicknamed me ‘The Bison’ because of my extraordinary physical power.
I won two Premier League titles, three FA Cups, a League Cup, and was an unused substitute in my club’s Champions League final victory.
My transfer to the Premier League cost £24.4 million, making me the most expensive signing in my new club’s history at the time.

I scored a spectacular late equaliser against Arsenal that was voted my club’s Goal of the Season. Two years later, I struck an unforgettable left-footed volley from 25 yards in a Champions League semi-final against Barcelona.
footballers.story: The Bison — a nickname that perfectly captured the raw, bulldozing power of one of the most complete midfielders the Premier League has seen. Essien arrived at Chelsea from Lyon for a then club-record £24.4 million in 2005 and became the engine room of Jose Mourinho’s dominant side. He could tackle like a defender, run like a sprinter, and shoot like a striker — his thunderous volley against Barcelona in the 2009 Champions League semi-final remains one of the great European goals. Knee injuries robbed him of his best years, and loan spells at Real Madrid and AC Milan saw diminishing returns. He finished his career with stints at Panathinaikos and Persib Bandung, far from Stamford Bridge — but at his peak, few midfielders on the planet could match him.
didYouKnow: His teammates nicknamed him The Bison because of his extraordinary physical power and ability to charge through midfields
His left-footed volley against Barcelona in the 2009 Champions League semi-final is widely regarded as one of the greatest goals in the competition's history
His £24.4 million transfer from Lyon in 2005 made him the most expensive African player in history at the time

footballers.name: Jimmy Bullard
clues: I was born on the 23rd of October 1978. I was called up to the England squad three times but never won a cap.
My career was plagued by knee injuries. I dislocated my kneecap after a tackle, missing 15 months. On my debut for my next club, I injured the same knee within 40 minutes.
Before turning professional, I worked as a painter and decorator while playing non-league football.
I was signed by West Ham, the club I supported, for just £30,000 — but never made a first-team appearance and was released on a free.
My career took me from non-league to the Premier League, from painting houses to playing at the highest level, but injuries meant I never quite fulfilled my potential.
footballers.story: The funniest man ever to play in the Premier League — and a genuinely brilliant footballer when his knees allowed it. Bullard worked as a painter and decorator while playing non-league football before West Ham signed him for £30,000, only to release him without a first-team appearance. Peterborough gave him a chance, and from there he climbed to Wigan, where he became one of the most creative midfielders in the division. A £2.5 million move to Fulham followed, and he was magnificent — but a horrific knee injury in 2006 robbed him of 15 months. Hull City paid £5 million for him and he repaid them with one of the greatest goal celebrations ever seen, recreating Phil Brown's infamous half-time team talk. Another knee injury on his Ipswich debut effectively ended his career. A cult hero and now a popular social media personality, Bullard is the ultimate what-if of English football.
footballers.numbers: 0 goals in 0 senior caps for England
didYouKnow: He worked as a painter and decorator while playing non-league football before turning professional in his twenties
His goal celebration at Manchester City — recreating Phil Brown's infamous half-time team talk by wagging his finger at his teammates in a circle — is one of the most iconic in Premier League history
He was called up to the England squad three times but never won a cap, with injuries always intervening at the worst moments

footballers.name: Kevin Phillips
clues: I was born on the 25th of July 1973. I played 8 times for my country, never scoring a goal.
I played for an extraordinary number of clubs across English football — more than most strikers could dream of.
I formed one of the Premier League’s great ‘little and large’ partnerships with a tall Irish target man — between us, we scored 77% of our club’s goals in one season.
I announced myself with a hat-trick in a 5–0 away win at Derby, during a season that saw me named Premier League Player of the Season.
In my greatest season I scored 30 Premier League goals — winning an award no Englishman has claimed before or since in the top flight of European football.
footballers.story: The only Englishman ever to win the European Golden Boot — and he did it at Sunderland, of all places. Phillips scored 30 Premier League goals in 1999-2000, forming a devastating partnership with Niall Quinn that fired the Black Cats to a seventh-place finish in their first season back in the top flight. Small, sharp, and ruthlessly clinical inside the box, he was the complete poacher. England somehow gave him only eight caps and never let him score. After leaving Sunderland he became a footballing nomad — Southampton, Aston Villa, West Brom, Birmingham, Blackpool, Crystal Palace, Leicester — racking up nine clubs in total, scoring goals at every single one. He scored the winning penalty in the 2011 League Cup final for Birmingham against Arsenal, one of the great cup upsets.
honours: Premier League Player of the Season (2000)
didYouKnow: He remains the only Englishman ever to win the European Golden Boot, scoring 30 Premier League goals for Sunderland in the 1999-2000 season
He played for nine different clubs across his career — scoring goals at every single one of them
He scored the winning penalty in the 2011 League Cup final for Birmingham City against Arsenal, one of the biggest cup upsets of the decade
